sentences.text: Yes.
When I came in the service, I called [INAUDIBLE], in-- this was in March, 1936.
And when we go, they start teaching us-- you see-- how's that-- how to fight, how to shoot, and everything.
The training, yes.
And after two weeks, comes the-- the leader from our platoon.
Even the captain from the Polish officers was very anti-Semitic.
But the platoon leader comes to me, and told me, Ignac, I am very proud that you coming to my platoon because you're one of the best.
Once was comes as they bring us every Friday to the synagogue, Jewish people.
The same thing like they bring the Catholics Sunday to the church.
It's Friday evening, only they bring it to the-- but this I like to notice.
When comes Passover, they bring us to the city.
And then the city organized for the Jewish soldiers a Passover evening.
When we come back to the [NON-ENGLISH], this is--
The barracks, a group of Polish soldiers-- a group of Polish soldiers start talking to teach the Jew a lesson because they wake us up in middle of the night.
And they start, you know that, to give our coats to throw over somebody into heat.
I see they start-- they come close to other two Jewish boys.
But they know me, too.
I, the first, jump out.
I take out my bayonet.
And I tell them, listen-- who will only touch somebody, I swear by God, and I swear by my uniform, will be killed.
And now you can do.
Or you go back in your places.
Or you like to attack with bayonets.
Everything was quiet.
They backed up.
I-- in my-- I don't know-- where I was born-- [INAUDIBLE], or this was I-- I don't know my individual--
Not to-- not to put my head down, but always up.
And even in my childhood, I remember my brother who was four years older than I.
When he was 12, I was eight.
And when he walked back from the school, he was running because two Polish boys are hitting him.
And he was running from them.
When I noticed that, I grab a stone.
And I got a [INAUDIBLE], and I fight with them.
He ran away.
But I was a [INAUDIBLE].
And this was-- I don't know if this is good or no good.
But this-- this is my life.
Yes, this is about the-- I give only, as a child, and then in the army.
Later on, they was the best friends with me.
They know that I know what I mean.
And I'm very honest.
Nothing-- only a [NON-ENGLISH], because I have not matured.
To have in Poland a rank, you must have eight class gymnasium.
If you have six, it is not enough.
You can never have a rank in the Polish-- in the Polish army.
She was waiting five years, and was ready for marriage because it was five years was going out.
She tell me, [INAUDIBLE] is this the time to marry, is we're married.
The wedding was not like here, you make a wedding.
It was a traditional wedding.
You see-- you have only the according to law of Moses, was the closest family and a few friends.
And it was not a honeymoon where to go.
You see, only we went, you see, a place where to stay, where to leave.
And I ordered the furniture, what I finish myself.
And I tell her we will have luxury-- a luxury home, not our home, a rented home.
But inside, it will be very luxury because I work by that.
And I will finish that.
A matter of fact, our furniture the Germans took it out during the war.
And they transport the furniture to Germany.
But up to now, I don't know where this is.
And-- so-- And then comes-- exactly, I come home in September for '37.
And in August '39 was the mobilization, the war, '39.
Yes.
Then the-- the war comes peace will be far away.
I feel it.
I feel the German propaganda.
I feel the Polish populations start taking over from the German the anti-Semitic ideas.
And I was afraid, because it is not-- in Germany, we have only 600,000 Jewish population.
This is a minimum.
But in Poland, 3.5 million population-- no, 35 million population-- we have 3-- no, Poland has-- wait a second.
Yes, we have 3.5 million Jewish population, this is 10%.
But if we understand Poland, it's not 10% from the Polacks, because was beaten the 35 million was.
So 5 million was raised White Russian, 7 million Ukrainians, and 3.5 million Jewish.
So the Poland was only around 20 million.
You see, but still, we feel depressed.
You see then everything is going-- like they start to break up Jewish business, not to buy by Jew, then other thing.
You start feeling-- it comes-- it is bad.
The situation is bad.
I could not describe how.
But you feel that.
Yes, I was still working.
Even I was-- when there was the mobilization, I was staying and working not far from where my wife was also working.
She comes in, crying.
You are staying here working.
It's mobilization they call you to the army-- this-- two days before the war, or four days before the war.
Yes, August '39.
Then I washed my hand.
And-- and I come home.
And then I said goodbye to my family, and my wife, and other friends.
They bring me to the train station, 1939, young.
And soon that I see that we have two, three minutes time, I jump out from the train.
I tell to my wife, please, Lonia, be very careful with the news-- because I have a good intuition.
My intuition is always very strongly.
I feel that I will be alive, but maybe very far away from you.
But be careful if you will not hear from me for a while.
Be careful, I am alive or not.
I feel I will survive-- I will be alive.
And then the I hear this, I must jump in the door, and goodbye.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I have the uniform, and we see the horrors from the war, how bad this is.
How people are killed.
The Germans-- high, technical with the tank, with the motorcycles, they cut the Polish territory with the Polish soldiers like-- how is-- like tomatoes.
Right away, in the second day of war, we have no connection with the army.
One unit lost the other unit because the telephone and the communication in the army was in the Germans hand, in German officer's hand.
What I was telling you was a lot.
And there was a lot of Polish officer from German ancestor.
And they right away, turn-- so we have no connection one unit with the other.
Then they can slice us like pieces of tomatoes.
Even they circle us around.
And-- yes-- And we were come as war prisoners.
I hear the command in German and in Polish.
I cannot tell what this is in English, But maybe you will help me.
[SPEAKING GERMAN] Polish soldiers and officers, throw away the guns.
You are war prisoners.
Then we are war prisoners.
I threw it away because I don't see the beginning from the soldiers.
I don't see-- yes, we got to keep [INAUDIBLE].
But we was fighting a little bit.
And we lost the contact.
And we can nothing do.
Maybe a week-- maybe a week.
So they try to transport us closer to the German territory, because the Polish territory is a large territory.
But the capital city was taken fighting, organized fight from the Polish capital city.
But-- is-- I like to tell you, then Warsaw was staying-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE].
They was staying two weeks, no more.
They was-- that Warsaw was keeping towards the time once it takes-- 23 days, they was fighting.
Even Paris, after 17 days was fallen.
But Poland is not so equipped like France, was fighting longer than France.
Yes, after 23, the capital.
And-- But the trains was no running in Poland.
We-- we walked like that.
On the march closer to the German-- to the Reich, to the German territory.
Then I will tell you.
On the second or the third day comes the first selection.
Was 16, one behind the other.
I cannot see the beginning, maybe 100,000, maybe 150,000 soldiers.
And they-- [NON-ENGLISH], so they-- to talk--
No, no, to talk loud, to call somebody.
How you call this?
When they call-- and they call out to give us a few minutes time.
They give us [SPEAKING GERMAN].
This mean, Polish soldiers, [SPEAKING GERMAN], who is from German ancestors, step out.
They stepped out.
From the other side comes truck.
It gives them right away them to eat, and to drink, to give them red armbands with sticks.
And you will have the control of this-- of this Polish band.
This was the first selection.
And then they throw us a piece-- a few breads.
And then--and we go.
The others, a day or two comes the other selection.
They give us a rest.
And they tell-- they call us out.
Polish soldiers, officers-- and maybe officers-- because they know not a lot Polish from Jewish ancestors, or Jews, step out.
I see a few hundred stepped out.
But meantime, as a war prisoner, I come back with a friend what I was in the same sport club in two, as a younger boy.
And we recognize each other.
And we start like it was together in this.
And so I grabbed two Jewish boys.
One name was Polivoda.
A matter of fact, his brother maybe is alive here in Toronto.
And the other was Dimenstein.
We was going together to the school, neighbors.
And I grabbed them.
And I tell them, don't go out.
And the other group who knows that I am Jewish, this is friend too, they tell, stay with us.
And they cover us.
But behind, I hear one of the Polacks, look-- and like to talk on the other boy, Dimenstein, this older are Jew.
And then the other from the two, quiet him down, and tell him, be quiet, because if not, I will quiet him down.
So everything was OK.
And the march route and march was very hard.
It was raining.
And sometimes when they give us a place to rest overnight, they put the machine guns on high so much, even it is raining, when you stand up, you hit first-- you are short.
Then you must be like that the whole night.
You don't know how long the machine guns are from shooting automatically.
Then I tell it to my friends, I plan to escape, because if I will come to the German territory, it will be very hard on the Polish territory.
I can help myself.
And I planned-- then Polivoda, he told me-- his brother is here, I think, in Toronto-- he tell me, you know, they will kill you like a dog.
You have no chance, I think.
Listen, if they torture-- if you take two persons, a righteous man and a man who is a criminal, when you give the same torture and the criminal type could be very strong.
But he will survive-- well-- could be very weak, but he will survive, because when he is a criminal, he knows that he is-- he is not correct.
He need the punishment.
I tell them, I will not wait.
When they torture me once, the second time.
And then when I will be killed like [INAUDIBLE], or not this man what I was born.
And I decide, I will take my luck.
If I will survive, it's OK.
It not, it's nothing.
A moment was a day we didn't water.
And on the road start-- how is that-- you know how in the small towns they--
A well, and they bring water up.
Everybody, thousands, bring-- to do with-- drink water, you see?
And I see I have 300 yard to go to the-- to the-- I see the trees and everything.
The forest.
Then I jump.
And I know, I have not a shot.
And then I run that way, that way.
And I come to the forest.
When I come to the forest, I know now I'm for myself.
Wit these two guys.
With these two guys.
Yes, the encounters is not only when you fight army against army, but as a war prisoner-- a short time, but I notice.
When they give us once a rest for a few minutes, then we're sitting a moment.
And they give the command-- and I notice by me sits a young boy, a young soldier, maybe only in service for the first two months.
And he's with a swollen leg.
And how he came-- how he walked, we don't know.
But when they give-- he could not more stand up.
And when they give the command, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
Stand up and go, march-- he can't.
And I give him my arm to help him.
I am stronger.
I see he is so young.
Then the German tell to me, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
I try, then the German not understand.
Then I speak a good German.
Then when he point with his gun, I step back.
But I am staying.
And I tell to the Polish soldier, show him your leg because you need medical help, because it was like a gangrene-- it was impossible.
Then he show him.
And the German very quiet, nice talking-- you can see it is educated.
Maybe it's high school, maybe even university.
A German, how he talk, he is not nervous.
You see, like the Slavic or the other nation, he took very quiet and very nice.
And he-- he look on this leg and tell him to [SPEAKING GERMAN].
Don't be childish, so nice word.
You cannot stay here.
You must go.
Then he look on me, the soldier-- I leap-- [GERMAN].
Then I see by the third time, he can shoot me.
And then I step back.
And the third time he tell, he must go.
When he show him, [GERMAN], he fell back.
He shoot him straight in the head, with this leg, kick him to the road-- march.
He didn't even point with the-- with his eye.
I thought this is [SPEAKING GERMAN] murders.
They can do everything like automatic.
Everything, if we'll beat this Englishman, he will be nervous.
If it will be [INAUDIBLE] he will kill, he will be nervous.
If it will be a Russian, he will be nervous.
He will give him a word for his mother, and his father.
But the German was, from the beginning till the end, quiet, fine, with a smile, nice words, act like a beast.
I don't know.
God forbid, God help us, from the cold-hearted murderers, because they can do everything.
The is right.
My intuition was right.
They will do it.
This was [INAUDIBLE].
And this gave me more than all the propaganda against the Germans.
This act gave me so much hatred to see that, the world, like they show it, you see?
And to escape.
And I escaped.
What happened when you went into the woods with your two friends?
When we went to the woods-- but I'm in full uniform.
And I was so afraid because when the German killed, then they will not take you the second time [INAUDIBLE].
They shoot you right away, because you are on the German-- it is already German territory.
But they are still on the head-- on the-- on the outer worlds and that.
But in the side roads, they was afraid still to come in.
Then I meet a man.
I saw was very nice dress, a cultured man.
And we start talking before.
We go to a farmer.
He give us something to take off at least a half uniform and to be half in civil and half in uniform.
And I talk with him.
And he tell me he run [INAUDIBLE] city.
He is professor [NON-ENGLISH] from [NON-ENGLISH] university-- from historical Krakow University, not-- a Christian.
And we were talking.
We are talking.
Then he ask me, tell me, who are you?
Be honest with me.
I tell him, I am Jewish.
Then he tell me, you know, the best way go on the path with the Russian occupied, because you know what the German are doing every day, if you are Jewish.
OK, I didn't know till you tell me.
Save your life.
Go there.
And then you will see what is what.
I'm rector of a university from historical university from Krakow.
But this is the true safe.
Then we decided where to go.
Dimenstein tell me like that-- his wife is supposed to give birth in this time.
Then he will go back.
What will be happen this happen to him?
He will go back.
The other, Polivoda, and tell me, you know, Ignac, I believe in you, what you will do, I will do it.
OK, let's go.
And decide to east Poland toward the Russian occupied.
Now it's take time to walk.
You see, we walk only-- we was hiding from the-- not go on the highways, but only on the side roads by the farmers.
And then I come to a place.
And I see already the Russian, because the Russian was going 125 kilometer more west.
And after they went back, they make the line between Germany and Russia.
And I see the little river.
But I see the Germans has take one side, the Russian the other side.
Yes, [NON-ENGLISH], I don't know.
The river is [NON-ENGLISH], but the place, I don't know, because I was going-- you see, I don't have-- I'm not a map and not a compass.
Because I threw it away, the compass and everything.
I know the men were going to the east.
And I see the river [NON-ENGLISH].
And the Germans told the farmers to take out the potatoes, because they will be frozen.
And they can have the potatoes, the Germans.
Then I look.
And I observe what the Germans are doing.
Then I take by a farmer also, I think I will take a hoe to--
Yes, to make-- and we going, and we took potatoes, potatoes, [INAUDIBLE].
Till we come to the-- close to the river, when I tell to Polivoda when we come close to the river [NON-ENGLISH].
It was 12 o'clock.
And the Germans was looking around for dinner.
We give one jump and we was on the other side.
It was a small river.
Middle, and then up.
And they cannot shoot anymore because this is-- I was already on the other side.
Then the Russian was so surprised.
And he tell, from where you come?
What you did?
I tell him.
I know a little bit Russian, because my father was in Russia.
So he speaks sometimes in the home Russian.
I told, listen, bring me to the headquarters.
From where you come?
I tell, I escape as war prisoner from the German [INAUDIBLE].
And tell me, you can talk Polish.
You can speak Russian.
How you know that?
I tell, my father was speaking Russian.
So I know.
He asked me, where I was, what kind of service.
And I tell them what.
Then I tell him I have a-- I mention the course dynamite specialist, and what I did in the time from the war, how long I was.
He tells, what kind schooling you have?
He asked me, what you are mobilized-- oh, you are [INAUDIBLE]?
No.
What is your trade.
I tell him, [NON-ENGLISH].
He don't believe me.
He tell me, show me your hands.
He was very smart.
I showed him my-- you are right or a left?
I'm a right.
He look at my fingers up to date.
This finger is thicker and shorter.
And this finger is longer and thinner.
These are from the trade.
Then he tell me, yes, you are honest.
And now there come officers.
What is to do with them, these two?
I tell them, let him free, go.
And we was working.
And tell me, you know what?
We go 125 kilometer back.
Two hours later, one hour later, will be here the German because the border will be the other rivers.
So we must go fast or the Germans will again catch you.
It's no good when they catch you the second time.
And we start walking about 10, 15 minutes, I don't know.
Behind, I hear the horn from a truck.
And the driver start yelling on us-- come up!
I don't know what he mean, because he was so yelling.
Then I turn over my pockets.
And I show him, I have nothing.
What is the rush?
Then he start the truck, grab me here, and tell me, the officer tell him to save us and to bring us to a bigger city because the Germans will catch us again.
We are young.
And he don't like when the Germans will catch us.
He bring us till the city of Lvov, Lemberg.
No, no.
Even when you are safe, they never tell you, because he tell you once.
Then he was serving on the border there between Poland and Russia.
For that, he know Polish, too.
He was surprised when I talk a little bit Russia.
And then-- then he asked me what I know from Russia.
I tell him, I know Tolstoy, Pushkin, or [NON-ENGLISH].
Then he was surprised, you see, that I know the Russian literature, even when I learned this in Polish.
And for that, maybe, was the-- he was so nice to us.
Yes, please.
